title: Pierogi
subtitle: Polish potato and cheese dumplings
excerpt: Soft dough parcels filled with mashed potato and cheddar, boiled until tender and finished in butter until lightly golden. The most beloved Polish comfort food, good enough to be the only thing on the plate. Makes about 24 dumplings; serves four.
sourceNotes: Pierogi are the national dish of Poland, made in every household in dozens of variations. The potato and cheese filling (ruskie, meaning Ruthenian) is the most popular. Documented by Anne Applebaum and Danielle Crittenden in From a Polish Country House Kitchen (2012) and by Anya von Bremzen in Mastering the Art of Soviet Cooking (2013).

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze after boiling, in a single layer on a tray, then transfer to bags. Cook from frozen in boiling water for 4–5 minutes, then fry in butter.
recipe.batchNotes: Make a double batch for the freezer — the extra effort is minimal once the filling and dough are made.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make the dough and filling up to a day ahead. Keep both refrigerated separately. Shape and boil just before serving.

content.text: Pierogi dough is forgiving — softer and more elastic than pasta, easy to work with, and very quick to bring together. The key is to rest it for 30 minutes before rolling; the gluten relaxes and the dough rolls thinly without shrinking back. The filling should be well seasoned and completely cold before shaping, or it will steam the dough from the inside and make the parcels wet.

items.prepNote: floury variety such as Maris Piper, peeled
finely chopped and fried golden in butter
finely sliced and caramelised, to serve

content.text: For the dough: combine {{plain-flour}} of flour with {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt in a bowl. Beat the egg with 150 ml of warm water and pour into the flour. Mix until a shaggy dough forms, then knead for 3–4 minutes until smooth and soft. Wrap and rest for 30 minutes. For the filling: boil the potatoes in salted water until completely tender. Drain and mash thoroughly — no lumps. Beat in {{cheddar}} of grated cheddar and the fried onion. Season generously with {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt and {{black-pepper}} of black pepper. Cool completely.

Roll the dough on a floured surface to about 3 mm thick. Cut circles about 8 cm across using a glass or cutter. Place a teaspoon of filling in the centre of each circle. Fold the dough over to make a half-moon, pressing the edges firmly to seal — pinch and fold the edge to make a pleated border. Bring a large pan of salted water to the boil. Cook the pierogi in batches for 3–4 minutes until they float and have been floating for 1 minute. Remove with a slotted spoon. Heat {{unsalted-butter}} of butter in a wide frying pan over medium heat until foaming. Fry the boiled pierogi in batches for 2 minutes per side until golden. Serve with caramelised onions and sour cream.

items.cause: Edges not sealed properly, or filling too wet.
items.fix: Pinch the edges very firmly, pressing all the air out before sealing. The filling must be dry — if the potato mash is wet, beat in more cheese.

items.cause: Not rested long enough after mixing.
items.fix: Rest for the full 30 minutes. If still shrinking, let it rest for another 15 minutes. Rested dough rolls to 3 mm easily.

items.cause: Potatoes not seasoned enough, or not enough cheese.
items.fix: Season the filling aggressively — the dough absorbs some of the salt. Taste the cold filling before using and adjust.

content.text: Pierogi are made on Christmas Eve and at Easter, at name days and family gatherings, and on any weekday when the mood requires something comforting. The fillings vary by region: potato and cheese in Ruthenia and eastern Poland, sauerkraut and mushroom in the west, sweet curd cheese and fruit in the south. The potato version called pierogi ruskie is the most common and the one that Polish diaspora communities around the world tend to carry with them. They were brought to the United States, Canada, and the United Kingdom in the 20th century by Polish immigrants and have become so familiar in those communities that they are simply referred to as Polish dumplings without further explanation.
